title: Loft and gapping: why your wedges must talk to each other
microFact: Ask any fitter: 'What's my gapping from PW to sand wedge?' That one question marks you as someone who gets it.
lesson:
LOFT is the clubface angle that launches the ball: a driver is ~9–12°, a 7-iron ~30°, a sand wedge ~56°. More loft = higher, shorter, more spin.

GAPPING is the spacing of lofts through your set so each club flies a distinct, evenly-stepped distance — ideally 10–15m between consecutive clubs. Modern game-improvement irons have strong (low) lofts for marketing distance, which quietly opens a chasm between your pitching wedge (~43–45°) and a 56° sand wedge — sometimes 30+ meters of no-man's-land where you have no club.

The fix: a GAP WEDGE (48–52°) between them. This is exactly the kind of detail a fitting session solves, and exactly the vocabulary that signals seriousness in a pro shop: "My PW is 44°, so I'm thinking a 50° gap wedge to fix the gapping — does that work with a 56?" 

Rule of thumb for your set: no two adjacent clubs should fly the same distance, and no gap should exceed ~20m.

visual.caption: Loft gapping: even steps club to club — and the PW→SW chasm a gap wedge fixes.

quiz.question: Your PW is 44° and your sand wedge is 56°. What's the classic fix for the distance gap between them?
quiz.explanation: A ~50° gap wedge splits the loft gap evenly, giving you a full-swing club for the in-between distance.
